flashcards.Back: Network Time Protocol. A protocol used to synchronize the clocks of computer systems and network devices over packet-switched, variable-latency data networks.
A hierarchical level that indicates the distance of a device from an authoritative time source. Stratum 0 is the highest precision clock, and the number increments with each hop.
High-precision timekeeping devices such as atomic clocks, GPS, or other radio clocks. They are not directly connected to the network but to Stratum 1 servers.
A network time server that is directly synchronized to a Stratum 0 device. it acts as a primary network time standard.
A configuration where a device requests time updates from an NTP server to keep its own internal clock accurate.
A configuration where a device provides time synchronization services to other clients on the network.
The Cisco IOS command used to specify an external time source for the local device to synchronize with.
A Cisco command that configures a device to act as an authoritative NTP server, using its own internal clock as the reference for other devices.
A verification command that displays the current synchronization state, the reference clock ID, and the local device's stratum level.
A verification command that lists all configured time servers and indicates which one is currently selected as the synchronization source.
The time difference between the local clock and the reference server's clock, measured in milliseconds.
The variability in the latency of time packets received from a server. High jitter can reduce the accuracy of synchronization.
An 8-bit octet shown in 'show ntp associations' that indicates the success rate of the last eight time queries (377 means all eight were successful).
A command that instructs a Cisco router to periodically update its hardware calendar chip with the time learned via NTP.
The IP address or string identifying the source that the local NTP process is currently synchronized to.
The status indicating that the local device has successfully matched its clock with a trusted NTP server and is within an acceptable margin of error.
A condition where a device has not yet established a reliable link with an NTP server or the time difference is too large to correct automatically.
A security feature that uses MD5 keys to ensure a client only accepts time updates from an authorized and verified server.
An NTP configuration where a server sends time updates to all devices on a local subnet simultaneously, rather than responding to individual requests.
The NTP value indicating that a device is unsynchronized and its time should not be trusted by other network nodes.
The battery-backed clock chip inside a router or switch that maintains time even when the device is powered off.
The internal timekeeping process maintained by the Cisco IOS kernel while the device is running. it is what NTP actually synchronizes.
The standardized port number used for all NTP communication between clients and servers.
The frequency at which an NTP client sends a request to its server. this interval increases as the clock stability improves.
A command used to specify a particular local interface (usually a Loopback) whose IP address should be used for all outgoing NTP packets.
The round-trip time measured for an NTP packet to travel from the client to the server and back, used to calculate the correct time.
The process of making very small, gradual adjustments to the local clock speed to bring it into synchronization without jumping the time forward or backward.
A relationship between two devices at the same stratum level where they exchange time information to improve the stability of their respective clocks.
The tendency of a hardware clock to gradually lose or gain time over a period due to environmental factors or crystal quality.
A one-second adjustment occasionally applied to UTC time to keep it synchronized with Earth's rotation. NTP can distribute info about upcoming leap seconds.
